---
title: "Manifesto Scoring Report"
author: Morgan Ballesteros
format: pdf
toc: false
---
```{r}
#| label: setup
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| echo: false

library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(readr)
library(stringr)

# Source (load) your function definitions from an .R script in the R/ folder.
# This ensures any functions you wrote in scripts are available in this Quarto session.
source("R/score_manifestos.R")

```

```{r}
#| label: import-processed
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| results: false

# Read the processed scoring output produced earlier (relative path inside your repo).
# show_col_types = FALSE prevents readr from printing the column type summary.
scores <- readr::read_csv("data/processed/manifesto_scores.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)

# Quick structural check for you while developing (column names/types, a few values).
# With results: false, this will not print in the PDF, but it *will* run.
dplyr::glimpse(scores)
```

```{r}
#| label: descriptives mean prevalence
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| results: false

# Compute mean prevalence for each marker across all documents.
# - group_by(marker): separate rows by marker (e.g., war/violence/attack)
# - summarize(): reduce each marker group to a single row (the mean prevalence)
# - na.rm = TRUE: ignore any missing prevalence values
# - .groups = "drop": return an ungrouped tibble after summarizing
by_cat <- scores %>%
  group_by(marker) %>%
  summarize(mean_prev = mean(prevalence, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

# Printing the object would show a tibble in the PDF; we suppress results above to keep the report clean.
by_cat

```

```{r}
#| label: plot1
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| fig-cap: "Mean prevalence by marker."

# Bar chart of mean prevalence by marker.
# - aes(x = marker, y = mean_prev): marker labels on x-axis; mean prevalence on y-axis
# - fill = marker: gives each bar a different fill color by marker
ggplot(by_cat, aes(x = marker, y = mean_prev, fill = marker)) + 
  geom_col() + 
  labs(x = "Marker", y = "Mean prevalence")

```

```{r}
#| label: join-categories
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false

# Read your manually-created metadata file that maps each doc_id to a category (violent/extreme/moderate).
doc_meta <- readr::read_csv("data/raw/doc_meta.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)

# Join the category labels onto the scored data.
# left_join keeps every row in scores and brings in category where doc_id matches.
# If doc_id doesn't match exactly, category would become NA.
scores_cat <- scores %>%
  dplyr::left_join(doc_meta, by = "doc_id")
```

```{r}
#| label: build-bycat
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false

# Compute mean prevalence by BOTH category and marker.
# This produces a table suitable for a faceted bar chart:
# rows represent category × marker combinations
# mean_prev is the average prevalence within each combination
by_cat <- scores_cat %>%
  dplyr::group_by(category, marker) %>%
  dplyr::summarize(mean_prev = mean(prevalence, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")
```

```{r}
#| label: plot2
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| fig-cap: "Mean prevalence (per 1,000 words) by category and marker."

# Faceted bar chart:
# - x = category: violent/extreme/moderate
# - y = mean_prev: mean prevalence in that category
# - facet_wrap(~ marker): one mini-plot per marker
# NOTE: scales="free_y" usually makes more sense here because prevalence ranges differ by marker.
# If you keep free_x, it won’t help with y-axis differences.
ggplot(by_cat, aes(x = category, y = mean_prev, fill = marker)) +
  geom_col() +
  facet_wrap(~ marker, scales = "free_x") +
  labs(x = "Category", y = "Mean prevalence (per 1,000 words)") +
  # Rotate/shrink x-axis labels so they don’t overlap in PDF output.
theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1, size = 7))

```

```{r}
#| label: descriptives-with-total-count
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false

# Build a small descriptive table by marker with:
# mean_prev: average prevalence across documents
# total_count: total raw count of marker hits across documents
desc_tbl <- scores %>%
  dplyr::group_by(marker) %>%
  dplyr::summarize(
    mean_prev   = mean(prevalence, na.rm = TRUE),
    total_count = sum(count, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

# Format for nicer presentation:
# arrange(desc(mean_prev)): show most prevalent markers first
# round(mean_prev, 3): cleaner numeric display
# as.integer(total_count): drop decimals if any appear
desc_tbl <- desc_tbl %>%
  dplyr::arrange(dplyr::desc(.data$mean_prev)) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    mean_prev   = round(.data$mean_prev, 3),
    total_count = as.integer(.data$total_count)
  )

# Print a clean PDF-friendly table using knitr::kable.
# col.names makes the table headers readable.
# caption adds a table caption in the rendered PDF.
knitr::kable(
  desc_tbl,
  col.names = c("Marker", "Mean prevalence", "Total count"),
  caption = "Marker descriptives across all documents."
)
```

```{r}
#| label: plot-prev
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| fig-cap: "Prevalence distribution by marker (per 1,000 words)."

# Violin plot shows the distribution of prevalence values for each marker across documents.
# fill = marker colors violins by marker
# stat_summary(fun = mean, geom = "point") overlays the mean as a dot on each violin
ggplot(scores, aes(x = marker, y = prevalence, fill = marker)) +
  geom_violin() +
   stat_summary(fun = mean, geom = "point") +
  labs(x = "Marker", y = "Prevalence")

```
